posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
As mentioned far downthread, I am thrilled to see such a high response rate this year. As I said, we ended up wtih 937 total entries.

For benchmarking, last year (out of 520 or so) the 1988 D’Yquem was first-place with 4 votes. 5 other wines received 3 votes each. In terms of house references, D’Yquem had 12, Selosse 10 and Mugnier and Keller 8 each.

Let’s trundle on to 2024

A repeat winner, the 2001 D’Yquem sits as the WOTY with 6 total votes. Right behind it with 5 votes is the 2006 Krug Clos du Mesnil and then Selosse Initial (various disgorgements) and the 2013 Cristal shared the show position with 4 votes each. A passle of other wines came in with 3, including some fun surprises.

  • 2006 Bollinger VV Francaise
  • 1982 Cheval Blanc
  • 1983 D’Yquem
  • 1982 Margaux
  • 2001 DRC La Tache
  • 2002 Emidio Pepe Montepulciano (!)
  • 2010 Sauzet Montrachet
  • 2023 Jasud Own Rooted Cab
  • 2018 L’Anglore Tavel Rose
  • 2005 Patty Green Notorious PN
  • 1996 Rousseau CSJ
  • 2001 Rousseau Clos de Beze
  • 2006 Taittinger CdC
  • Ulysse Collin Les Perrieres

In terms of house representation, Krug was tops with 26 total references. Rousseau took 2nd place there with 22 and D’Yquem trails them with 19. The others who garnered 8 or more

Cedric Bouchard – 14
Cheval Blanc – 10
Coche-Dury – 9
DRC – 12
Drouhin – 9
Dujac – 11 (highest ever for them, I think)
Emidio Pepe – 8 (ditto for sure)
Mugneret-Gibourg – 14 (ditto with a cherry and chinaware on top!)
Goodfellow – 8
Huet – 12 (with I believe the widest range, from 1919 to 2010)
Keller – 10
Roederer – 12
Roumier – 8
Selosse – 10
Taittinger – 11
Ulysse Collin – 10
Lopez de Heredia – 9 (1 Bosconia, 1 Tondonia red and the rest Tondonia white)

Some other stuff…

Well, PYCM and Salon were both a little lower than I’d expected with 7 each. As happens, not that many Calicabs. No Bryant, No Abreu, No Kapscandy. Only one Rivers-Marie. And in the non-Cab part of things, no Kongsgaard, no Littorai of any stripe, one Marcassin. Ridge did OK with 7 mentions

A real surprise. Only one Latour. Interesting! Only one Egon Muller. No Angelus. 4 Prums and 3 Donnhoffs, which are both lower numbers than I might have thought. And Supertuscans didn’t show up—zero Masseto, zero Tig, zero Sassicaia. Only a couple of Juge and Allemand. Only one La-La. And one Vega. Only one port. And only one Dom mention

It was fun to see Cecile Tremblay with a strong 6-vote showing. Ditto Goodfellow on a strong showing and nice to see a couple mentions of Extradimensional Wine Co Yeah! Yay Larry and Tercero as well.

Last comment. Ruchottes Chambertin is something a lot of people got to talk about this time. I think the vineyard ended up with 11 (between Rousseau and M-G)

But more than anything, the spectrum of wines listed was far and away the widest I’ve ever seen. It’s super-energizing to see the diversity in our community. On to 2025!

& & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & & &

Hey—no sassing. I waited all the way till the beginning of November!

As always, feel free to jump on with your respective lists. This has already been an excellent year for me (although not yet with any true soul-drenching-standout) and that’s without the major road trip in a couple of weeks. I expect there to be a number of additions. We’ll see if anything displaces the Prager.

I haven’t had my Discovery of the Year, but I’m sure I will this coming Sunday. That’s when I’ll be at a dinner at which a 1715 Madeira will be opened. I feel privileged and very fortunate to get to try. Edit—Yup. The 1715 Terrantez Madeira is DEFINITELY the DOTY

As usual, I have provided a score only where my tasting note on the wine prompted that extra level of “description”. Also as usual, I will do the tabulations in the first week of January. Also, if there are any other WOTY threads already started (e.g. halfway-through-the-year), please feel free to link them so that I can add any wines in those threads to the tabulations. Thanks all!

I’ve found retailers/importers who I trust and want to build a lasting relationship with. Several bottles in my top 3’s was enjoyed with members of this board.

At a recent dinner at Blue Hill we tried to order two late 80s Stony Hill Chardonnays which were both oxidized. posts.name: Andrew Hamilton
posts.cooked:
Currently my list is as follows, although I expect later in the month things will change as we’ve got a ‘baller bottle’ lunch planned. But for now:

White - 2011 Bouchard Meursault Perrieres
Red - 1967 Produttori Riserva Speciale Barbaresco

Both of these bottles were consumed during a black truffle lunch in July. Unsurprisingly the Krug showed very well, as did the 71 Produttori. But the 67 definitely was a step above the 71.

image1920×1440 291 KB

image1920×1440 242 KB

posts.name: Sh@n Agra
posts.cooked:
1989 Lynch Bages - The first wow Bordeaux I’ve had, and incredibly consistent.
2020 DRC RSV - The first DRC I had. Brutally young, but I could feel the stuffing and balance. I didn’t know DRC were so polished, and it somewhat explains why there is such mass appeal.
2020 Figeac - Great wine with all sorts of complexity. Kind of reminded me of the polish and concentration of the DRC, which I thought spoke to QPR well (i.e. this wine being 1/10th the price).
2016 Cappellano Rupestris. This wine is intense. Big aromatics, big fruit, big tannin, big acids - all in poise. Also big alcohol (label 15%). A WWE wrestler in a ballerina costume. Will be hauntingly good at maturity… reminds me of larger scaled 2001.
2012 Giuseppe Rinaldi Brunate. This may be the best Barolo I’ve ever had. In this zone of medium bodied fruit and savory complexity. Just really superb right now. Definitely feels older than its age would have suggested.

Discovery of the year:
Hofgut Falkenstein Gisela. Just waves of intense flavors and complexity. Awesome QPR and a really fun wine.

posts.name: Lars Carlberg
posts.cooked: Gisela was voted best 2023 Kabinett in Germany by Vinum Weinguide Deutschland.

These wines and the Panos Brane Cantenac tasting I went to rekindled some interest in Bordeaux that had waned for a number of years.

Also, have not had a Tokaji in many years under we had lunch at Maison D’Estournal when we were in Bordeaux. Had a 2010 Tokaj-Hétszölö 5 puttanos that was really good. The winery apparently is owned by the people that own Cos D’Estournal. Tokaj-Hétszölö, a Hungarian legend

posts.name: Bob Hoelting

2024 has been a year of value plays, keeping my avg bottle cost <$30. Paul C would be proud I’m not including anything that didn’t come from my cellar, like all the killer juice available at our too-infrequent offlines.

My top two wines thus far are products of none other than our own Larry Schaffer:

2015 Tercero Syrah White Hawk Vineyard
2014 Tercero Mourvèdre Larner Vineyard
I rated both of these 95

Filling out my top 9, all rated 94 pts:
2005 Château Rieussec
2018 Sandler Wine Company Syrah Nolan Vineyard
2022 Brian David Chardonnay
2019 de Négoce Cabernet Sauvignon OG N.200
2015 Goodfellow Family Cellars Pinot Noir Heritage No. 5
2013 Jean-Marie Massonnot Champagne Brut Millésimé

2019 Domaine de L’Idylle Mondeuse Vin de Savoie l’Identite d’un Terroir

That Mondeuse was definitely my discovery of the year… great stuff.

So fragrant - red fruits, warm red brick, honey like flowers - with a palate reflective of those notes, as well as some blood orange building off of lively acidity.

posts.name: Mads Wildgaard
posts.cooked: That’s amazing. Stoked for you. Will have a look and see if I can find some —

Confirming that the 1715 Terrantez Madeira is definitely the DOTY this year
